title: To terminate the designation of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan as a major non-NATO ally, and for other purposes.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.
plain_summary: This bill removes Pakistan's special status as a major non-NATO ally of the United States right away. Being a major non-NATO ally gives countries special benefits like getting extra military supplies from America and working together on defense projects. Pakistan can only get this special status back if the President says Pakistan has done enough to fight against a terrorist group called the Haqqani Network. Pakistan would need to attack this group, stop them from using Pakistan as a safe place to hide, work with Afghanistan to control their shared border, and arrest the group's leaders.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Termination of Designation of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan as a Major Non-NATO Ally
sections.text: Starting on the day this law takes effect, Pakistan will no longer be considered a major non-NATO ally (a special status that gives certain countries military and economic benefits even though they are not part of NATO). The President cannot give Pakistan this special status again unless the President can promise Congress that Pakistan is doing four specific things. First, Pakistan must continue military actions that seriously disrupt the safe places and ability to move around freely of a terrorist group called the Haqqani Network inside Pakistan. Second, Pakistan must take steps to show it is committed to stopping the Haqqani Network from using any part of Pakistani territory as a safe place to hide. Third, the Government of Pakistan must actively work with the Government of Afghanistan to limit the movement of militants like the Haqqani Network along the border between Afghanistan and Pakistan. Fourth, Pakistan must show progress in arresting and prosecuting senior leaders and middle-level members of the Haqqani Network.
